subConcepts.name: Counteractive dynamical system
subConcepts.summary: The functional target of any ecology of practices: a set of mutually reinforcing psycho-technologies that, once internalized, takes on a self-rolling life of its own and can resist the self-organizing force of parasitic processing.

passages.phrase: what you want to do is you want to cultivate a counteractive dynamical system. See, Parasitic Processing is a very complex dynamical system, and if you try and do one shot interventions, it just reconfigures itself.

subConcepts.name: Complementary and checks-and-balances relationships
subConcepts.summary: An ecology of practices is not an arbitrary collection but a set of practices whose strengths and weaknesses mutually correct one another, producing reliable self-complexification.

passages.phrase: you should be setting up psychotechnologies, sets of practices and cognitive styles that have complimentary relationships to each other, that have sets of corresponding checks and balances, strengths, and weaknesses.

subConcepts.name: Sapiential community as necessary condition
subConcepts.summary: No ecology of practices can be safely sustained in isolation; it requires a community that provides corrective feedback and prevents dangerous autodidactic spirituality.

passages.phrase: people are forced, as I said, to cobble together in a dangerously autodidactic fashion, their own personal responses to perennial problems without traditions, guidance, communities, well-worked out sets of practices, well-vetted, well-developed.

commonConfusions: An ecology of practices is not simply a personal routine or habit stack; it must be a dynamical system that functions collectively across levels of cognition, character, and consciousness.
Ecology of practices does not mean any combination of self-chosen techniques; Vervaeke insists the practices must have complementary relationships and mutual checks, not just coexist.
The ecology is not optional insurance on top of normal life; it is the primary structural response to perennial problems that are themselves dynamical and self-reinforcing.

keyPassages.phrase: cultures – and individuals, communities, at multiple scales – what people have developed is they have developed sets of psycho-technologies, ecologies of practices that help alleviate the suffering of these perennial problems.

what you want to do is you want to cultivate a counteractive dynamical system. See, Parasitic Processing is a very complex dynamical system, and if you try and do one shot interventions, it just reconfigures itself.

what you're getting there is a strong recommendation for looking at this as cultivating an ecology of practices, getting sets of practices, sets of psycho-technologies that have complementary relationships to each other, organizing them together.

The historical factors lead to the undermining of the whole ecology of practices and psycho-technologies and cognitive cultural grammar that people have created in order to respond to the perennial problems.
having a ecology of psychotechnologies for addressing the perennial problems is necessary, but, of course, not sufficient for responding to the meaning crisis.

subConcepts.name: Enactive analogy as the vehicle of gnosis
subConcepts.summary: Gnosis works through enacted, not merely described, analogies that bridge the world one is in and the world one aspires to, creating a liminal participatory state in which perspectival and participatory knowing can shift.

passages.phrase: the enactive analogy has to be similar enough to the world and the person you're trying to become in that world. It has to be similar enough that you can feel it.

subConcepts.name: Altered state of consciousness as empowering gnosis
subConcepts.summary: The ritual context of gnosis is always powered by some altered or higher state of consciousness that increases cognitive flexibility, making the enactive analogy and anagoge possible.

passages.phrase: the psychedelics give you the cognitive flexibility in the flow state and the possibility even of a higher state of consciousness that then empowers this process to get you free from existential entrapment.

subConcepts.name: Gnosis distinguished from Gnosticism
subConcepts.summary: Gnosis is a salvific mode of participatory knowing available to cognitive science; Gnosticism is a historical mythological movement that gnosis can be salvaged from without inheriting its conspiracy-prone dualism.

passages.phrase: Gnosis is to have a set of psychotechnologies that create a ritual context like Jeepform, like martial arts, like therapy that allows us to overcome being existentially stuck, existentially stupefied.

commonConfusions: Gnosis is not a body of secret knowledge or hidden propositions; it is a transformative mode of knowing that changes participatory and perspectival knowing, not the propositional layer.
Gnosis is not the same as Gnosticism; Vervaeke explicitly wants to salvage the former while rejecting the latter's dualistic mythology and conspiracy-prone tendencies.
Gnosis is not a single dramatic moment; it requires a constructed ritual context, enactive analogy plus enactive anagoge, that must be carefully crafted with significant skill.
Gnosis is not therapy redescribed; therapy is one modern instance of gnosis, but gnosis is the broader category that includes martial arts, Jeepform, ritual, and contemplative traditions.

keyPassages.phrase: Gnosis is to have a set of psychotechnologies that create a ritual context like Jeepform, like martial arts, like therapy that allows us to overcome being existentially stuck, existentially stupefied.

what I'm doing is I'm setting this within a ritual context where I'm at, I'm feeling enactive analogy and I'm doing enactive anagoge. I'm doing serious play and I have the flexibility, the cognitive flexibility.

gnosis frees me from being existentially trapped. It's this combination, this integration of psychotechnologies that activate and transform perspectival and participatory knowing and give us a sense of a greater reality that we want to live within

That enacted ritual should also afford anagoge. It should afford the transframing, the reciprocal anagogic process by which self and world are transformed such that we can go through the sensibility transcendence, which will make a worldview viable to us.
we can respond to existential entrapment by the cultivation of gnosis, which can be empowered by a core capacity for realizing higher states of consciousness.

subConcepts.name: Sati: remembering the being-mode
subConcepts.summary: The original Pali meaning of mindfulness (sati) is not paying attention to the present moment but re-membering, recovering, the being-mode of existence, which modal confusion causes us to forget.

passages.phrase: Siddhārtha is trying to remember (writes sati on the board) the being mode. It's in the eyes of the renouncer.

subConcepts.name: Attentional scaling and cognitive flexibility
subConcepts.summary: Mindfulness trains the capacity to scale attention both up (contemplation, Metta) and down (meditation, Vipassana) and to flow between them, producing the cognitive flexibility needed for insight and frame-breaking.

passages.phrase: Mindfulness is basically teaching us how to appropriate and train a flexibility of attentional scaling so that we can intervene effectively in how we are framing our problems and increase the chances of insight when insight is needed.

subConcepts.name: Opponent processing: meditation and contemplation
subConcepts.summary: Mindfulness is not synonymous with meditation alone; it coordinates meditation (scaling down, de-automating) and contemplation (scaling up, making new patterns) in opponent processing so that optimal attentional grip is dynamically maintained.

passages.phrase: you want to train people in both of these skills and then train them to flow between them. It's called 'oponent processing'. So they're pulling and pushing on each other and so they're forced to coordinate and constantly get the right degree of attentional engagement

commonConfusions: Mindfulness is not simply paying attention to the present moment in a non-judgmental way; Vervaeke insists this pedagogical language conceals the explanatory mechanism, which is attentional scaling flexibility for increasing insight.

Mindfulness is not synonymous with meditation; meditation (scaling down) is only one half; contemplation (scaling up) is equally essential, and the coordination between them is what produces cognitive flexibility.
Sati does not mean mere present-moment awareness; it means recovering the being-mode of participatory existence that modal confusion causes us to forget.
Mindfulness is not a relaxation technique; its core function is to increase the probability of insight by training the capacity to break and rebuild frames.

keyPassages.phrase: Siddhārtha is trying to remember (writes sati on the board) the being mode. It's in the eyes of the renouncer.

Mindfulness is basically teaching us how to appropriate and train a flexibility of attentional scaling so that we can intervene effectively in how we are framing our problems and increase the chances of insight when insight is needed.
you want to train people in both of these skills and then train them to flow between them. It's called 'oponent processing'. So they're pulling and pushing on each other
we can address modal confusion by the cultivation of Sati.

you shouldn't equate mindfulness just with meditation. It's not. So if you pay attention, for example, to the eight fold path you'll have people being trained in meditative practices, contemplative practices, practices in which you flow between the opposites

subConcepts.name: Separating meaning from physics
subConcepts.summary: The point of premeditatio is not morbidity but the cognitive skill of distinguishing the existential meaning one is co-creating with a loved one from the physical facts of mortality, which lie outside one's control.

passages.phrase: When you're kissing your child good night, say to yourself, "I may lose them to death tonight!". Because you have to learn to distinguish the meaning from the physics.

commonConfusions: Premeditatio malorum is not morbid pessimism or rehearsing catastrophe; it is a targeted psycho-technology for decoupling existential meaning from physical outcomes so that love is held without fragile dependence on permanence.

The practice is not about expecting bad events; it is about training the prosoche-level capacity to identify what is and is not within one's power (hegemonic faculty) in an affectively charged context.

Premeditatio is not Stoic resignation to fate; it is a practice that, paradoxically, deepens rather than diminishes appreciation, because it forces conscious engagement with what is genuinely valuable.

keyPassages.phrase: When you're kissing your child good night, say to yourself, "I may lose them to death tonight!". Because you have to learn to distinguish the meaning from the physics.
You have tremendous control over the meaning that you and your son are making together. You have very little control over the physics of his mortality.
PROSOCHE is to pay attention. Now, obviously we're always paying attention, so that's useless advice! What they meant is "pay attention to how you're paying attention"
we can address modal confusion by the cultivation of Sati.

subConcepts.name: Attending to how one is judging
subConcepts.summary: Prosoche is specifically attention to one's judging, framing, and meaning-assigning, learning to see the difference between the event and the existential significance one is projecting onto it.

passages.phrase: pay attention to how you're paying attention, pay attention to how you're judging, pay attention to this process. Learn to see there's a difference between the meaning and the event.

commonConfusions: Prosoche is not simply being more attentive to the world; it is meta-attention, attending to the process by which one is assigning salience and meaning, not to the content of the world itself.
Prosoche is not identical to mindfulness; it is a specific Stoic application that foregrounds the co-identification of agent and arena, whereas sati foregrounds recovery of the being-mode.
The Stoics are not saying to become detached or unemotional; prosoche teaches discrimination between what is in one's power (the meaning one makes) and what is not (the event).

keyPassages.phrase: PROSOCHE is to pay attention. Now, obviously we're always paying attention, so that's useless advice! What they meant is "pay attention to how you're paying attention"

pay attention to how you're judging, pay attention to this process. Learn to see there's a difference between the meaning - and what I mean here, the modal meaning, the existential mode you're in - learn to distinguish between the meaning and the event.
What's another practice that the Stoics engage in? A practice they engaged in is called "the view from above".
we can address modal confusion by the cultivation of Sati.

subConcepts.name: Exaptation from physical tools to cognitive tools
subConcepts.summary: A psycho-technology is the cognitive analogue of a physical tool: just as a bottle fits the hand and enhances carrying, literacy fits the brain's language capacity and enhances reflection; the move from physical to cognitive is exaptation.

passages.phrase: A psycho-technology fits your brain and enhances how it operates.

subConcepts.name: Internalization into second-order thinking
subConcepts.summary: The distinctive power of psycho-technologies is that they can be internalized, moving from external scaffolding to internal metacognitive capacity, producing second-order thinking and the ability to scrutinize one's own cognition.

passages.phrase: I start to get a capacity for what Robert Bellah calls "second order thinking".

subConcepts.name: Domain-generality as the criterion
subConcepts.summary: A true psycho-technology must be applicable in a domain-general manner, extending beyond the specific context in which it was learned, which is why literacy and numeracy qualify but narrow vocational skills do not.

passages.phrase: The psychotechnology is a socially generated and standardized way of formatting, manipulating, and enhancing information processing that's readily internalizable into human cognition, and that can be applied in a domain general manner.

commonConfusions: A psycho-technology is not any technique or trick; it must be standardized, learnable across a population, and domain-general, meaning it reshapes cognition across many contexts.
Psycho-technologies are not simply beliefs or ideologies; they are practices that, when internalized, actually restructure how the brain processes information, not merely what it believes.

Psycho-technologies can become invisible through familiarity; literacy is so universal that people forget it is a culturally constructed cognitive device, which obscures how radical its effects on cognition are.

keyPassages.phrase: A psycho-technology fits your brain and enhances how it operates.

The psychotechnology is a socially generated and standardized way of formatting, manipulating, and enhancing information processing that's readily internalizable into human cognition, and that can be applied in a domain general manner.
Way before the Internet networked computers together, culture networked brains together in order to provide some of our most powerful problem solving abilities.
I start to get a capacity for what Robert Bellah calls "second order thinking".

cultures – and individuals, communities, at multiple scales – what people have developed is they have developed sets of psycho-technologies, ecologies of practices that help alleviate the suffering of these perennial problems.

subConcepts.name: Credo always in service of religio
subConcepts.summary: The religion that is not a religion never privileges propositional creed over the participatory binding of religio; any criterion-setting mythos must remain open-ended and revisable in service of ongoing relevance realization.

passages.phrase: a way of thinking about that is the religion that's not a religion would always, always have credo in the service of religio.

subConcepts.name: Co-operative network of communities of practice
subConcepts.summary: The structural form of the religion that is not a religion is a co-operative network of communities of practice in dynamic reflective equilibrium with a wisdom wiki, allowing both top-down research and bottom-up practitioner feedback.

passages.phrase: we have a co-op network of communities of practice. I already talked about what that is and that that is in a reflective equilibrium, dynamic, ongoing one with a wisdom wiki.

subConcepts.name: Targeting consciousness, cognition, character, and culture
subConcepts.summary: The religion that is not a religion aims at the comprehensive transformation Vervaeke names the 4 Cs: consciousness, cognition, character, and culture, the same scope that historical religions addressed but now grounded in cognitive science.

passages.phrase: We need a comprehensive set of psychotechnologies that are set within communities of practices that allow for the comprehensive transformations of consciousness, cognition, character, and culture in a way that is analogous to religion

commonConfusions: Religion that is not a religion is not simply secular humanism or atheism; it must functionally do what religion did, activate religio, address perennial problems, and afford transformative experience, without supernatural metaphysical commitments.
It is not a proposal to found a new movement or institution; Vervaeke explicitly says he is pointing to something people are already building and contributing to a dialogue, not issuing a manifesto.

It is not opposed to existing religious traditions; creedal religion is respected for particular people, and the project is dialogical with figures like Tillich, Jung, Corbin, and Barfield who worked within traditions.

keyPassages.phrase: we get that religion that's not a religion? And then, put that into dialogue with other people who have engaged in similar project, people like Tillich and Jung and Corbin and Barfield.

We need a comprehensive set of psychotechnologies that are set within communities of practices that allow for the comprehensive transformations of consciousness, cognition, character, and culture in a way that is analogous to religion
a way of thinking about that is the religion that's not a religion would always, always have credo in the service of religio.
we have a co-op network of communities of practice. I already talked about what that is and that that is in a reflective equilibrium, dynamic, ongoing one with a wisdom wiki.
We seem to need a religion that cannot be any kind of religion at all. We can't deal with the Meaning Crisis by pursuing pseudo-religious ideologies, trying to deal with this as a political issue.

subConcepts.name: Enlightenment as reliable amelioration of perennial problems
subConcepts.summary: Vervaeke stipulates that enlightenment is not an unachievable superlative but the developmental process that reliably ameliorates and alleviates the perennial problems, making it a tractable scientific and practical target.

passages.phrase: I'm going to stipulate that enlightenment is the developmental process that gives us reliable amelioration and alleviation of the perennial problems.

subConcepts.name: Mapping perennial problems to specific practices
subConcepts.summary: The reverse-engineering project involves systematically identifying which psycho-technologies address which perennial problems: sati for modal confusion, flow for the reflectiveness gap, Prajna for absurdity, communitas for alienation, gnosis for existential entrapment.

passages.phrase: we can address parasitic processing with a counteractive dynamical system. We can address modal confusion by the cultivation of Sati. We can address the reflectiveness gap by the cultivation of flow.

subConcepts.name: Wisdom framing as the overarching integration
subConcepts.summary: Each response to a perennial problem must be set within a wisdom framing, interlocking sets of virtues that together produce reliable enlightenment, not merely a grab-bag of techniques.

passages.phrase: When this is set within a wisdom framing so that comprehensively that person is developing interlocking sets of virtues for addressing self-deception and for affording self-optimization and this results in a reliable response of amelioration and alleviation of the perennial problems, I would say that's enlightenment

subConcepts.name: Naturalistic grounding via relevance realization and religio
subConcepts.summary: The reverse-engineering project uses the cognitive-scientific machinery of relevance realization, religio, and sacredness to provide a naturalistic theoretical foundation for what practices do and why they work.

passages.phrase: I want to reverse-engineer enlightenment. I want to understand what the perennial problems are and using this machinery (indicates RR, Religio, Sacredness), what are the practices, what are the processes we can use in order to address the perennial problems?

subConcepts.name: Two-pronged response: worldview rearticulation and ecology of practices
subConcepts.summary: Awakening from the meaning crisis requires both rearticulating a worldview in which religio is legitimated and cultivating an ecology of psycho-technologies for the perennial problems, neither alone is sufficient.

passages.phrase: So responding to the Meaning Crisis has two components to it. And that's why I call it 'awakening' from the Meaning Crisis

commonConfusions: Reverse-engineering enlightenment is not proposing a theory of enlightenment as an object but a method: starting with the target (alleviation of perennial problems) and working backward to identify the practices and mechanisms.

The project is not claiming to achieve or describe final enlightenment; Vervaeke explicitly calls it a developmental process, not an achieved superlative state, and acknowledges it is not the final answer.

This is not a secularization that discards the wisdom traditions; it is a cognitive-scientific reframing that tries to salvage the functional machinery of those traditions while grounding them in a naturalistic worldview.
Reverse-engineering enlightenment is not a solo project; it requires the social and institutional structures of the religion that is not a religion to be sustainable.

keyPassages.phrase: I want to reverse-engineer enlightenment. I want to understand what the perennial problems are and using this machinery (indicates RR, Religio, Sacredness), what are the practices, what are the processes we can use in order to address the perennial problems?
I'm going to stipulate that enlightenment is the developmental process that gives us reliable amelioration and alleviation of the perennial problems.

we can address parasitic processing with a counteractive dynamical system. We can address modal confusion by the cultivation of Sati. We can address the reflectiveness gap by the cultivation of flow. We can address absurdity by cultivating Prajñā.

When this is set within a wisdom framing so that comprehensively that person is developing interlocking sets of virtues for addressing self-deception and for affording self-optimization and this results in a reliable response of amelioration and alleviation of the perennial problems, I would say that's enlightenment

insofar as we can give an account of this in terms that are ultimately naturalistic, that can be subject to scientific investigation, we will have a scientific theory of enlightenment and what it can mean for us.

subConcepts.name: Ritual as enacted pattern, not symbolic representation
subConcepts.summary: Ritual is patterns of interaction, not merely patterns of representation; by enacting the analogy rather than merely symbolizing it, the participant can get the perspectival and participatory knowing that words and beliefs cannot convey.

passages.phrase: Ritual means enactive analogy, enactive Anagoge. It means the processes by which I try to activate the machinery of transformation. So when I'm saying 'mythos', don't just hear 'patterns of representation' hear 'patterns of interaction'

subConcepts.name: Ritual as liminal play between two worlds
subConcepts.summary: Ritual places the participant in a liminal state between the current self-world and the aspired self-world, making the comparison phenomenologically available in a way that enables rather than forces transformation.

passages.phrase: ritual properly understood is people are playing serious play in order to try and put themselves into a liminal place, a place between two worlds, the normal world and the sacred world.

commonConfusions: Ritual is not neurotic routine; Vervaeke explicitly distinguishes them: neurotic routine is compulsive re-enactment, whereas ritual is serious play directed at genuine transformative aspiration.

Ritual is not merely symbolic or decorative; it is enactive analogy, the participation in the actions is what enables the perspectival and participatory knowing that mere belief or symbol cannot produce.
Modern practices like therapy, Tai Chi, and Jeepform are ritual in Vervaeke's technical sense even though they are not traditionally called religious.

keyPassages.phrase: Ritual means enactive analogy, enactive Anagoge. It means the processes by which I try to activate the machinery of transformation.
ritual properly understood is people are playing serious play in order to try and put themselves into a liminal place, a place between two worlds, the normal world and the sacred world.

Religious ritual was a way of playing with enactive analogies so that I can compare so I could overcome the ignorance. I can see B, these two worlds, these two ways of being, these two persons within those worlds.
You have this ritual behavior. Again, like when I'm doing Tai Chi, like Tai Chi, right? These are all, this is enactive analogy.

That enacted ritual should also afford anagoge. It should afford the transframing, the reciprocal anagogic process by which self and world are transformed such that we can go through the sensibility transcendence

subConcepts.name: Liminal dwelling between two selves and worlds
subConcepts.summary: Serious play places one in a liminal state that is neither the current world nor the aspired world but between them, a space in which both can be held simultaneously and compared perspectivally and participatorily.

passages.phrase: ritual properly understood is people are playing serious play in order to try and put themselves into a liminal place, a place between two worlds, the normal world and the sacred world.

commonConfusions: Serious play is not entertainment or casual game-playing; the 'serious' marks that the player is genuinely aspiring to transformative change while the 'play' marks that they are not yet fully committed to the new world, both are essential.

Serious play is not pretending; it uses the structural features of play (suspension of ordinary reality, reversibility, trying out) as a psycho-technology for confronting transformative experience without being overwhelmed.
Serious play does not require formal ritual settings; therapy, martial arts, role-playing games like Jeepform, and contemplative practice all constitute serious play in Vervaeke's sense.

keyPassages.phrase: are people doing in all these instances there? They're confronting the possibility of a transformative experience. And so what they do is they have an enactive analogy.
it's not fun. It's not frivolous. It's serious play. Pretend your mom is there and enact that.
One of the things we need to do. As you can probably see an implication of what I'm saying as part of addressing the meaning crisis is we have to recover play

what I'm doing is I'm setting this within a ritual context where I'm at, I'm feeling enactive analogy and I'm doing enactive anagoge. I'm doing serious play and I have the flexibility, the cognitive flexibility.

subConcepts.name: 'Right' as optimal fittedness, not moral righteousness
subConcepts.summary: Each element of the eightfold path is 'right' in the sense of right-handedness or dexterity, optimal fittedness in relevance realization, not moral purity, which grounds the cognitive-scientific reinterpretation.

passages.phrase: Remember that the right is not moral righteousness; the right is right-handedness, it's dexterity. And now to use language that we've developed: it's right fittedness, it's optimal fittedness.

subConcepts.name: Self-rolling wheel: interdependence of the eight limbs
subConcepts.summary: The eight elements are not independent practices to be taken up singly; they are designed as an interdependent system that, once cultivated together, starts to self-generate momentum like a rolling wheel.

passages.phrase: what you have is a set of practices that are interdependent with each other, mutually supporting and self-rolling, becoming a self-rolling wheel.

commonConfusions: The eightfold path is not a sequential checklist to be completed in order; all eight limbs function simultaneously as an interdependent dynamical system.

'Right' in the eightfold path does not mean morally correct in a rule-following sense; it means optimally fitted, dexterous, in relevance realization at each level of cognition, character, and consciousness.

The eightfold path is not uniquely Buddhist in its function; Vervaeke treats it as the paradigm case of a counteractive dynamical system, not as a religious doctrine requiring Buddhist metaphysical commitments.

keyPassages.phrase: The Eightfold Path is a counteractive dynamical system that counteracts parasitic processing and does reciprocal opening, beyond the ego self and beyond the everyday world where it's represented by an eight spoked wheel.
Remember that the right is not moral righteousness; the right is right-handedness, it's dexterity. And now to use language that we've developed: it's right fittedness, it's optimal fittedness.
what you have is a set of practices that are interdependent with each other, mutually supporting and self-rolling, becoming a self-rolling wheel.
the cultivation of the eightfold path in Buddhism, which is very, very perspicaciously represented by an eight spoke wheel; the integration, it revolves, it evolves
It's supposed to be a self-organising system that rolls itself in which each part is interdependent on all the other parts.

subConcepts.name: The Solomon effect: third-person de-centering
subConcepts.summary: The view from above exploits the Solomon effect: moving from first-person to third-person perspective on one's own situation reliably produces de-centering insights, as research on Solomon's paradox demonstrates.

passages.phrase: What's another practice that the Stoics engage in? A practice they engaged in is called "the view from above". You can see Marcus Aurelius doing it in the meditations. And think about the "Solomon Effect" that we talked about: moving from the first person perspective to the third person perspective.

commonConfusions: The view from above is not escapism or detachment from life; it is a calibrated change in level of construal, seeing the same situation from a broader vantage, that produces insight while returning one to the situation more wisely.
It is not the same as resignation or stoic indifference; the practice shifts perspective to clarify what genuinely matters, not to eliminate caring.
The practice works because it is a psycho-technology exploiting a known cognitive mechanism (the Solomon effect / construal level theory), not because of any mystical elevation.

keyPassages.phrase: What's another practice that the Stoics engage in? A practice they engaged in is called "the view from above".
you're viewing some situation and you're enmeshed in it. Now view it 'I'm higher up in space and time', and then higher up still in space

moving from the first person perspective to the third person perspective. And there's all kinds of evidence about altering your level of construal, having these very powerful effects on your cognition and your sense of self.
practices like that can be drawn from stoicism. Like the view from above or objective seeing can help us to remember Sati, the being-mode.

subConcepts.name: Proleptic rationality
subConcepts.summary: Aspiration must be a form of rationality even though it cannot be inferential; Callard calls this proleptic rationality, the rationality appropriate to aspiring toward values one does not yet fully possess, otherwise aspiring to rationality itself would be irrational.

passages.phrase: My aspiring to rationality has to be itself a kind of rationality. That's proleptic rationality, or to use something older. My loving of wisdom. My aspiring to becoming wise cannot itself be an irrational process.

subConcepts.name: Incremental aspiration vs. sudden inspiration
subConcepts.summary: Callard's aspiration is the incremental developmental complement to L.A. Paul's sudden transformative experience; both require perspectival and participatory knowing inaccessible to inferential decision-theory.

passages.phrase: Agnes Callard in her book Aspiration has recently argued that there are also instances where people go through this transformative knowing that are much more incremental in nature.

commonConfusions: Aspiration is not mere motivation or wanting; it is a specific structure in which one proleptically acts on values one does not yet possess, using placeholders (symbols, gnosis) to bridge the gap.
The fact that aspiration cannot be solved by decision theory does not make it irrational; Callard's central point, which Vervaeke endorses, is that it is a form of proleptic rationality.
Aspiration is not the same as inspiration; inspiration (sudden insight-driven transformation) and aspiration (incremental developmental trajectory) are complementary, not competing, accounts.

keyPassages.phrase: My aspiring to rationality has to be itself a kind of rationality. That's proleptic rationality, or to use something older. My loving of wisdom. My aspiring to becoming wise cannot itself be an irrational process.
Agnes Callard in her book Aspiration has recently argued that there are also instances where people go through this transformative knowing that are much more incremental in nature.
She argues that there's a form of rationality appropriate to aspiration. She calls it proleptic rationality.

aspiration also probably has an affective component to it. And I suggested that wonder, and we saw how central wonder is to the cultivation of wisdom, that wonder is the affective state that's most conducive to aspirational progress

Wisdom is an ecology of psychotechnologies and cognitive styles that dynamically enhance relevance realization in inference, insight and intuition, internalization, understanding, and gnosis, transformation, and aspiration.

subConcepts.name: Knowing as conformity: taking on the idos
subConcepts.summary: Participatory knowing is grounded in Aristotle's conformity theory: to know something is to share the same structural-functional organization with it, so that knowing changes one's very being.

passages.phrase: So when I know some object or know some thing, my mind takes on the same structural functional organization as the thing such that if I could take that idos from my mind and actualize it in some potential, I could make an instance of the thing

subConcepts.name: Participatory knowing as the foundational kind
subConcepts.summary: All other kinds of knowing, perspectival, procedural, propositional, depend asymmetrically on participatory knowing; without the agent-arena attunement that participatory knowing constitutes, the other kinds cannot function.

passages.phrase: the perspectival ultimately depends on the participatory. Now of course it goes this way, right? They affect each other in multiple interactions, I was not originally drawing the arrow of causal interaction, I just did that, but what I was trying to draw originally was the arrow of dependence: asymmetric dependence.

subConcepts.name: Participatory knowing enacted in shamanism and gnosis
subConcepts.summary: When the shaman becomes the deer, or when therapy enacts the transformative analogy, what is being activated is participatory knowing: knowing by identifying with, not merely knowing about.

passages.phrase: the shaman is enacting the animal, the shaman isn't having beliefs about the deer. The shaman is becoming the deer. I don't mean metaphysically, but the shaman is trying to get together the sense of the skills, the kind of perspective the deer has, the way the deer thinks

commonConfusions: Participatory knowing is not mere emotional involvement or 'feeling connected'; it is a specific epistemic structure in which the knower and the known mutually constitute each other through conformity of structural-functional organization.
Participatory knowing is not mystical or non-cognitive; Vervaeke grounds it in Aristotle's conformity theory and in 4E cognitive science, it is the most foundational level of the cognitive hierarchy.
Participatory knowing is not independent of other kinds; it is the foundation on which perspectival, procedural, and propositional knowing rest, undermining it undermines all of them.
Participatory knowing is not accessible through propositions alone; precisely because it requires identity-with, not representation-of, it must be cultivated through gnosis, ritual, and practice.

keyPassages.phrase: There's the kind of knowing that is knowing what it's like to be in something you're participating in like a relationship.

the perspectival ultimately depends on the participatory. Now of course it goes this way, right? They affect each other in multiple interactions, what I was trying to draw originally was the arrow of dependence: asymmetric dependence.
the shaman is enacting the animal, the shaman isn't having beliefs about the deer. The shaman is becoming the deer.
That's what you're doing in therapy. Pretend your mom is there and enact that. Get the analogy, talk to her.

I want to propose a term to you. I want to argue that relevance is in this sense, transjective. It is a real relationship between the organism and its environment. We should not think of it as being projected. We should not think of it as being detected.

subConcepts.name: Salience landscape as the content of perspectival knowing
subConcepts.summary: Perspectival knowing is constituted by one's salience landscape, what stands out and recedes, which is why transformative experiences that alter the salience landscape directly alter what it is like to be the person.

passages.phrase: Part of what's happening, part of what consciousness is doing, is it's creating a salience landscape for me.

subConcepts.name: Loss of perspectival knowing in the scientific revolution
subConcepts.summary: Pascal diagnosed modernity's collapse of the spirit of finesse (perspectival and participatory knowing) into the spirit of geometry, leaving modern people without the knowing needed for transformative experience.

passages.phrase: And so Pascal is pointing out that what has been lost in the scientific revolution is all these other kinds of knowing and being, and these are the kinds of knowing and being that he found present in the transformative experience that he had.

commonConfusions: Perspectival knowing is not subjective bias; it is a genuine kind of knowing constituted by what it is like to occupy a perspective, which is why it cannot be replaced by propositional reports about one's perspective.
Perspectival knowing is not the same as emotional response; it is the structural situational awareness, the salience landscape, that makes emotional response intelligible in the first place.
Perspectival knowing is not unalterable given; transformative experiences, mindfulness practice, and gnosis can systematically alter one's perspectival knowing, that is precisely their point.

keyPassages.phrase: There's the kind of knowing of what it is like to be having this experience right now.

And so Pascal is pointing out that what has been lost in the scientific revolution is all these other kinds of knowing and being, and these are the kinds of knowing and being that he found present in the transformative experience that he had.
the perspectival ultimately depends on the participatory. Now of course it goes this way, right?
It's at the level of procedural and ultimately perspectival and participatory knowing.
you don't know what it is like cause that's the core of you don't know what your salience landscape will be like when you eat this fruit until you have eaten the fruit.

subConcepts.name: Knowing-how and implicit learning
subConcepts.summary: Procedural knowing is the knowing instantiated in skills and powered by implicit learning, the non-conscious pickup of complex statistical patterns, which is why it cannot be replaced by propositional instruction.

passages.phrase: This has to do with your capacity for implicit learning.

subConcepts.name: Procedural knowing depends on perspectival
subConcepts.summary: Skill application requires situational awareness, one must know what it is like to be in this situation in order to apply the right skill at the right moment, making procedural knowing asymmetrically dependent on perspectival knowing.

passages.phrase: your procedural abilities. James doesn't use this language, but I could find passages in James that clearly point to it, I would argue, that your propositional knowing has to be grounded in your procedural abilities, which have to be grounded in your perspectival, which has to be grounded ultimately in your participatory.

commonConfusions: Procedural knowing is not mere muscle memory or automatic reflex; it involves a complex coordination of implicit learning, situational awareness, and skill application that is inaccessible to propositional description.
Knowing how is not a lesser kind of knowing than knowing that; the dependency hierarchy shows that propositional knowing actually depends on procedural knowing, not vice versa.

Procedural knowing cannot be acquired through instruction alone; one must practice, make mistakes, and allow implicit learning to operate, which is why no amount of propositional knowledge about riding a bicycle teaches one to ride.

keyPassages.phrase: There's the kind of knowing that's involved in knowing how to catch a baseball.
This has to do with your capacity for implicit learning.
we have slowly lost the importance of procedural knowledge - knowing how to do things; we've lost perspectival knowing - knowing what it's like; and we've lost participatory knowing
It's at the level of procedural and ultimately perspectival and participatory knowing.
your propositional knowing has to be grounded in your procedural abilities, which have to be grounded in your perspectival, which has to be grounded ultimately in your participatory.

commonConfusions: Propositional knowing is not the highest or most foundational kind of knowing; it is the most derivative, depending on procedural, perspectival, and participatory knowing beneath it.

The modern reduction of all knowing to propositional knowing is not a neutral epistemic choice; it is itself a historically produced outcome of the meaning crisis that has left modern people without the other three kinds.

Having correct propositional knowledge about a practice (e.g., mindfulness) does not substitute for actually doing the practice; the propositional and procedural/perspectival/participatory are irreducibly different.
Propositional knowing is not useless in Vervaeke's account; it is genuinely valuable but must be grounded in and responsive to the lower-order knowings rather than replacing them.

keyPassages.phrase: We tend to have reduced all of the ways that the ancients talked about how we know to one thing: To know is to have a special kind of belief.
There is much more to knowing than having justified true beliefs.
you do not have to be transformed in order to come into contact with ultimate reality. All you have to do is use the right method, do the right computation.
It's at the level of procedural and ultimately perspectival and participatory knowing.

we are getting a lot about how we should think about Relevance Realisation where we should look for it and notice it's starting to give us a way of connecting and thinking about the four kinds of knowing.

subConcepts.name: Bi-directionality: self and world transforming together
subConcepts.summary: Sensibility transcendence is not a one-way change in how one sees the world; it is a simultaneous co-transformation of the perceiver and the perceived, the mother-in-law and the daughter-in-law both become something they could not be before.

passages.phrase: Wright calls this Sensibility Transcendence. Because what's happening, simultaneously, is the daughter-in-law can be something that she couldn't be before and the mother-in-law is becoming somebody she couldn't become before; she couldn't be before. These two things are happening together.

commonConfusions: Sensibility transcendence is not a single-direction change in perspective or attitude; it is fundamentally bi-directional, both self and world are transformed together, which is what makes it participatory rather than merely propositional.

It is not the same as reframing; reframing changes how one sees a specific problem, while sensibility transcendence transforms the entire framing process, the style of sensibility, which is why Vervaeke calls it transframing.
Sensibility transcendence is not voluntary in the way changing a belief is; it requires the structural conditions of gnosis, enactive analogy, anagoge, and altered consciousness, to make it possible.

keyPassages.phrase: Wright calls this Sensibility Transcendence. Because what's happening, simultaneously, is the daughter-in-law can be something that she couldn't be before and the mother-in-law is becoming somebody she couldn't become before; she couldn't be before.
It's not a reframing of a particular problem. It's a transformation of the whole framing process: Both ends.
she's not only reframing how she sees the world, she's reframing how she sees herself and these are happening in a completely interfused manner. This is a Participatory Change.
in order to make a worldview viable to me, I have to go through this self world sensibility transcendence. So what I need is I need not only an enactive analogy, I need a way of enacting anagoge.

I start to see aspects in the balance that I hadn't seen before, precisely because of how sensitized I am to the symbol. Not only seeing her daughter-in-law differently, but seeing her own seeing differently; that trans-framing.

subConcepts.name: Asymmetric dependence hierarchy
subConcepts.summary: The four kinds of knowing form a hierarchy of asymmetric dependence: propositional depends on procedural, procedural on perspectival, perspectival on participatory, but none reduces to any other, and the reduction can only go upward.

passages.phrase: what I was trying to draw originally was the arrow of dependence: asymmetric dependence. This depends on this, this depends on this, this ultimately depends on this.

subConcepts.name: Modern collapse into propositional knowing
subConcepts.summary: The meaning crisis is partly constituted by the modern collapse of all knowing into the propositional, which erases the procedural, perspectival, and participatory knowings that are essential to wisdom, self-transcendence, and meaning.

passages.phrase: We tend to have reduced all of the ways that the ancients talked about how we know to one thing: To know is to have a special kind of belief. And so we are very belief centric which is why we are so focused as a culture on ideologies.

subConcepts.name: The four kinds as the vehicle of meaning-making
subConcepts.summary: Because relevance realization operates below the propositional level, at the procedural, perspectival, and participatory strata, recovering these kinds of knowing is essential to any genuine response to the meaning crisis.

passages.phrase: It's at the level of procedural and ultimately perspectival and participatory knowing.

commonConfusions: The four kinds of knowing are not four ways of doing the same thing; they are irreducibly different kinds with different cognitive mechanisms, different developmental requirements, and different failure modes.
The hierarchy of dependence does not mean the lower kinds are less valuable; participatory knowing is the most foundational, the highest in the dependency chain, not the lowest in value.

Recovery of procedural, perspectival, and participatory knowing is not accomplished by learning propositions about them; each must be cultivated through appropriate practice, which is why an ecology of psycho-technologies is needed.

The four kinds are not a historical curiosity from ancient Greece; Vervaeke grounds them in contemporary cognitive science, they map onto implicit learning (procedural), salience landscaping (perspectival), and 4E cognition (participatory).

keyPassages.phrase: We tend to have reduced all of the ways that the ancients talked about how we know to one thing: To know is to have a special kind of belief. And so we are very belief centric which is why we are so focused as a culture on ideologies.
what I was trying to draw originally was the arrow of dependence: asymmetric dependence. This depends on this, this depends on this, this ultimately depends on this.

we have slowly lost the importance of procedural knowledge - knowing how to do things; we've lost perspectival knowing - knowing what it's like; and we've lost participatory knowing - knowing that is part and parcel of how we are bound up with something else, someone else, in a process of mutual transformation
It's at the level of procedural and ultimately perspectival and participatory knowing.

we are getting a lot about how we should think about Relevance Realisation where we should look for it and notice it's starting to give us a way of connecting and thinking about the four kinds of knowing.

subConcepts.name: Epistemic and personal transformation
subConcepts.summary: A transformative experience involves both epistemic transformation (changing what you know, what it is like) and personal transformation (changing who you are, your participatory knowing), which is why no inference or decision procedure can take you across the gap.

passages.phrase: you don't know what it is like cause that's the core of you don't know what your salience landscape will be like when you eat this fruit until you have eaten the fruit. There's no way of knowing that ahead of time.

subConcepts.name: The vampire example: confronting the logic of transformative ignorance
subConcepts.summary: L.A. Paul's vampire thought experiment makes vivid the logic of transformative experience: one cannot know what one will lose or gain without going through the change, yet everything is at stake, producing a genuine rationally irresolvable impasse.

passages.phrase: I don't know what I'm going to lose if I become a vampire. I don't know what I'm going to lose. Once I go through this change, I will have lost a way of being.

subConcepts.name: Gnosis as the practical response to transformative ignorance
subConcepts.summary: Because transformative experience cannot be reasoned through propositionally, the practical response is gnosis, enactive analogy plus enactive anagoge, which allows one to get a perspectival and participatory taste of the aspired world without fully committing.

passages.phrase: So the way to think about this is what are people doing in all these instances there? They're confronting the possibility of a transformative experience. And so what they do is they have an enactive analogy.

commonConfusions: Transformative experience does not mean any dramatic life event; Paul's technical meaning requires both epistemic transformation (changing what it is like to be you) and personal transformation (changing who you are) simultaneously.

The irrationality of transformative experience is not a license for recklessness; Vervaeke's point is that the appropriate response is gnosis and serious play, getting enactive taste of the aspired state, not arbitrary leaping.

Transformative experience is not only available through mystical or religious routes; L.A. Paul's examples include ordinary life-transforming decisions (having a child, major career changes), and Vervaeke extends this to any worldview change.

keyPassages.phrase: you don't know what it is like cause that's the core of you don't know what your salience landscape will be like when you eat this fruit until you have eaten the fruit.
I don't know what I'm going to lose if I become a vampire. I don't know what I'm going to lose. Once I go through this change, I will have lost a way of being.

So the way to think about this is what are people doing in all these instances there? They're confronting the possibility of a transformative experience. And so what they do is they have an enactive analogy.

That combination, that ritual combination of enacted analogy and enactive anagoge is empowered by the cognitive flexibility brought on by an altered state of consciousness, hopefully a higher state of consciousness that gives us a sense of the increased realness.

These are awakening experiences. These are experiences in which people come back from the mystical experience and say 'that was somehow more real than this and I need to change my world. I need to change myself'.
